summary: 11 concrete tools and sites surfaced from HN comments.

items.name: Regent
items.source_story_title: Show HN: Git for AI Agents
items.summary: Version control for AI agent activity — tracks why agents changed a repo.
items.why_included: Show HN introduced a clear workflow layer for rewinding and bisecting agent sessions.
items.evidence: Commenters called it “Version Control for AI agent activity” and discussed tracing deletions and rewinding compacted sessions.

items.name: Mojo
items.summary: A Python-like systems language for high-performance AI and heterogeneous compute.
items.why_included: The beta drew sustained discussion around Python interop and the open-source roadmap.
items.evidence: Comments focused on Python interop, the planned open-source release, and whether Mojo can replace bottlenecks without rewriting code.

items.name: Meshtastic
items.summary: LoRa mesh messaging for off-grid text comms without internet or power.
items.why_included: The thread praised its emergency and remote-use cases despite bandwidth limits.
items.evidence: Commenters described it as off-grid messaging, compared it with MeshCore, and suggested cheap boards plus antennas.

items.name: MeshCore
items.summary: A secure off-grid mesh communications system with a companion app.
items.why_included: HN treated it as a serious alternative to Meshtastic for static repeater setups.
items.evidence: Comments contrasted MeshCore’s static and dynamic layers with Meshtastic’s flood-routing model.

items.name: Reticulum
items.summary: An open-source networking stack for resilient low-bandwidth mesh networks.
items.why_included: Commenters repeatedly pointed to it as an adjacent mesh-networking option.
items.evidence: The Meshtastic thread quoted Reticulum as a secure, decentralized stack that can run over readily available hardware.

items.name: CreepJS
items.source_story_title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
items.summary: A browser fingerprinting demo that probes how uniquely your browser identifies itself.
items.why_included: The privacy thread used it as a stronger comparison than the showcased page.
items.evidence: Commenters explicitly listed CreepJS alongside better fingerprinting demos.

items.name: BrowserLeaks
items.source_story_title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
items.summary: A suite of browser privacy leak tests covering IP, WebRTC, DNS, and more.
items.why_included: It was one of the main concrete alternatives cited for checking browser exposure.
items.evidence: HN commenters compared the demo page to BrowserLeaks when discussing what browsers reveal automatically.

items.name: Cover Your Tracks
items.source_story_title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
items.summary: EFF’s browser tracking test for fingerprinting resistance.
items.why_included: The thread repeatedly used it as the benchmark privacy test.
items.evidence: Commenters referenced Cover Your Tracks as a more useful, privacy-focused fingerprinting comparison.

items.name: Am I Unique?
items.source_story_title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
items.summary: A browser fingerprint diversity test that shows how identifiable your browser is.
items.why_included: It surfaced as another direct comparator in the fingerprinting discussion.
items.evidence: The HN discussion grouped Am I Unique with other fingerprinting tests like BrowserLeaks and ipleak.

items.name: IPLeak full report
items.source_story_title: A web page that shows you everything the browser told it without asking
items.summary: An IP/DNS/WebRTC leak test for checking what a site can infer.
items.why_included: It rounded out the privacy-testing tools named in the thread.
items.evidence: Commenters cited the ipleak full report alongside BrowserLeaks and Cover Your Tracks as a comparable leak test.

items.name: Flashpoint archive
items.summary: A preservation archive for old Flash games and web-era childhood classics.
items.why_included: The Cartoon Network thread explicitly recommended it as the place to find more preserved games.
items.evidence: A commenter told readers to check out the Flashpoint archive for more archived flash games.
